# Preface {.unnumbered}

Traditional business intelligence --- dashboards, static reports, and fixed queries --- is being replaced by AI agents that understand your questions, query your data, and deliver answers in seconds.  This book gives professionals from any background a hands-on understanding of how agentic AI transforms analysis, reporting, and decision-making.  No coding experience is required.

Chapter 1 explains what AI agents are and why they are displacing dashboards as the primary interface between decision-makers and data.  It lays out the conceptual foundation --- the agent loop, tool use, and the shift from fixed reports to conversational intelligence --- that the rest of the book builds on.

Chapter 2 introduces the tools.  You will install a coding agent, learn how the major options differ, and understand the environments where AI can execute code.

Chapter 3 shows how to query data and generate visualizations and reports with plain English.  You will open a coding agent in a folder containing a spreadsheet and go from raw data to a written recommendation with charts in under ten minutes.

Chapter 4 shows how AI can help you think through decisions.  You will use AI as a sparring partner --- challenging assumptions, running pre-mortems, and stress-testing your own analysis from Chapter 3.

Chapter 5 shows how AI can be connected to your company's databases, so anyone with proper credentials can query them in plain English.  You will learn about MCP connectors, build a reusable "skill" that encodes a database schema, and build a demo web app that anyone can use from a browser.

Chapter 6 shows how to automate multi-step workflows.  You will build a reporting pipeline that queries a database, generates charts, writes summaries, and assembles a PowerPoint deck --- all from a single instruction.

Chapter 7 shows how to use AI to query your company's documents and return answers grounded in your documents (with citations) rather than relying on its general knowledge.  This is called Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

Chapter 8 addresses reliability and compliance.  You will validate AI outputs, red-team skills and RAG systems, and build the governance framework your organization needs before deploying any of this.


## Data files

The exercises in this book use three datasets.

The [Superstore CSV](files/data/superstore.csv) contains roughly 10,000 retail orders across four U.S. regions with columns for sales, profit, discount, product category, and customer segment.^[The Sample Superstore dataset is published on Kaggle at <https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/vivek468/superstore-dataset-final>.  It is widely used in data-visualization tutorials and Tableau exercises.]  It is used throughout Chapters 3, 4, and 8.

The [Employee Attrition CSV](files/data/employee-attrition.csv) contains 1,470 employee records with 35 attributes including department, job role, monthly income, overtime status, job satisfaction, and whether the employee left the company.^[The IBM HR Analytics Employee Attrition & Performance dataset is a fictional dataset created by IBM data scientists, published on Kaggle at <https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/pavansubhasht/ibm-hr-analytics-attrition-dataset>.]  It is used in Chapter 3 exercises.

The [Chinook database](files/data/chinook.db) is a SQLite file representing a music retailer's operations with 11 related tables covering employees, customers, invoices, tracks, albums, artists, and genres.^[The Chinook database was created by Luis Rocha as a modern alternative to the Northwind sample database.  The source repository is at <https://github.com/lerocha/chinook-database>.]  It is the primary dataset for Chapter 5 and is revisited in Chapters 6 and 8.

Chapter 7 uses a [sample company handbook](files/data/company-handbook.md) for RAG exercises.

## Prerequisites

You need the Claude Desktop app installed on your machine.  Chapter 2 walks through installation and introduces the **Code** tab --- the interface we use throughout this book.  No prior coding or data science experience is assumed.

The examples use Claude Code via the Desktop app, but the concepts and workflows apply equally to alternative coding agents like Gemini CLI and OpenAI Codex, which Chapter 2 also describes.
